title: Plying from a centre-pull ball
subtitle: How to ply two strands from a single bobbin by winding a centre-pull ball
excerpt: When you have only one bobbin of singles and no spare bobbin to wind onto, a centre-pull ball lets you ply the yarn back on itself. This technique covers winding the ball and plying from both ends at the wheel.
sourceNotes: Centre-pull ball plying is a standard single-bobbin plying method widely used by handspinners. Technique synthesised from general spinning practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A ball of yarn wound so that one end feeds from the centre and the other from the outside, allowing both ends to be drawn simultaneously for plying.
A single strand of yarn produced by one pass through the spinning wheel, before any plying takes place.
A yarn made by twisting two singles together in the direction opposite to the spinning direction, producing a balanced, round yarn.
A plied yarn in which the ply twist exactly cancels the singles twist, producing a yarn that hangs straight without kinking.

aliases: ply from centre pull ball wheel

content.text: is useful when you have one full bobbin of

and no second bobbin to ply from. The yarn is wound into a ball so that one end comes from the centre and the other from the outside. At the wheel, both ends are drawn simultaneously and plied together in the S-direction, producing a
yarn from a single bobbin. The result is a true
provided the ball is wound with consistent tension throughout.

items.name: Small basket or bowl (to hold the ball during plying)

content.text: Hold the free end of the singles from the bobbin between two fingers of your non-dominant hand. Wind the yarn around those fingers in a figure-of-eight pattern 10 to 12 times to build a starting bundle. Pinch the bundle firmly and begin winding around it diagonally, rotating the bundle slightly with each wrap. Keep the tension consistent throughout: too loose and the ball collapses at the centre; too tight and the yarn distorts. Wind until the entire bobbin has transferred to the ball, then tuck the outside end under the last few wraps.

The centre end is the one you held between your fingers at the start; it sits loose inside the ball at the cast-on point. Feel inside from the underside of the ball and draw it out gently. The outside end is the one tucked under the last wraps on the surface. Place the ball in a small basket or bowl so it does not roll across the floor while plying.

Set the wheel to S-twist (opposite to the Z-twist of the original singles). Hold both strands together: the centre strand and the outside strand. Treadle at a moderate, even pace and allow the two strands to twist together. Keep both strands under equal tension by holding them at the same draft length between your hands and the orifice. If one strand feeds faster than the other it will bunch up at the join; ease the faster strand back by releasing it slightly while maintaining tension on the other.

Cut a 30 cm length of plied yarn periodically and hold both ends 15 cm apart, allowing it to hang freely. A balanced ply hangs without kinking. If it kinks toward S, reduce ply twist by treadling more slowly relative to how quickly you feed the yarn onto the bobbin. If it kinks toward Z, add more ply twist. Wind the finished yarn onto the niddy-noddy, soak in warm water for 20 minutes, then hang to dry under light tension to set the twist.

items.problem: The two strands feed at different rates and one bunches up
items.cause: The centre strand and outside strand are under unequal tension because the ball was wound unevenly
items.fix: Rewind the ball with even, consistent wraps. Hold both strands between the same set of fingers at the orifice so they feed together under identical control.

items.problem: The centre of the ball collapses mid-ply and the inner strand tangles
items.cause: The initial finger bundle was wound too loosely before the outer wraps were added
items.fix: Ease the tangle out gently without pulling sharply. For the next ball, compress the centre bundle firmly before beginning the outer wraps.

items.problem: The finished yarn kinks in the S-direction when a length is cut and allowed to hang
items.cause: Too much ply twist was added relative to the singles twist
items.fix: Increase the ratio of yarn fed to wheel rotations so less twist enters per length of yarn. Test a short section before winding the full skein.
